<!-- generated by build-digest.py --face review — do not edit by hand -->

# Codex Review Digest

Check-face of recurring Codex-caught defects. Cite any entry that applies.

## P — Plan checks

### P1
Grep the codebase for every named symbol the plan touches. If it isn't there, the plan is describing code from memory, not reality.

### P2
Open the actual definition for every API the plan calls. Codesight/tree-sitter node names and SDK signatures are the usual offenders — confirm, don't recall.

### P3
Run `--help` (or check the CLI source) for every command the plan tells someone to run. Flags drift; verify the exact invocation.

### P4
Trace each variable/param named in the plan to its declaration. A name that "sounds right" but isn't there is a round-1 finding.

### P30
For any plan that REUSES or EXTENDS an existing helper/utility/provider/table, open its BODY (not just its signature): (1) is every parameter actually USED inside the fn? (grep the param name in the body — an accepted-but-ignored arg is the tell); (2) does the return value VARY with outcome? (a mutex `acquire` must be able to return false; a `find` that always returns the first row; a guard that always passes); (3) does the backing DDL PERMIT the new use? — open the `CREATE TABLE`/constraints; a `CHECK`/`UNIQUE`/`PK (id=1)` may forbid it, and if the DDL lives in a DEPLOYED migration it is immutable (needs a NEW migration, never an edit); (4) does an existing TEST pin the current (possibly broken) behavior? — reuse means updating that test. "It already exists with the right signature" is P1/P2-clean but is NOT proof the body honors the contract its name advertises. Prefer a self-contained implementation over reusing a helper whose body you have not read. Pairs with P1 (phantom symbol — there the symbol is ABSENT; here it's PRESENT but lying) and C9 (type-valid-but-degenerate value).

### P31
For any `Option`-returning per-unit metric whose aggregate skips `None`, enumerate WHY a unit can be `None` and split the reasons into "out-of-scope / not-applicable" (correctly excluded) vs "in-scope but scored zero / failed / empty" (must be COUNTED as a zero). If the code infers this from the empty output (`count==0`, empty collection, a null dependency), it conflates them — add an EXPLICIT eligibility flag carried from where capability is known (the producer/trait), and decide zero-vs-N/A on that flag. Check EVERY path that produces the empty output — success-with-nothing, error-fallback, and any "no data" short-circuit — they are siblings of the same inflation.

### P32
For any "X does not exist / there is no established pattern for Y, so we'll create it fresh" claim, verify the ABSENCE with the same rigor as a presence (P1): don't trust one `find`/`grep` (wrong root, over-broad `-prune`, a different dir). Confirm by asking the CONSUMER — "how does a SIBLING test/module already load this kind of resource?" Grep for the capability (`include_str!`, fixture loaders, the file extension) across the WHOLE repo, not one subtree. If a convention exists, the plan MIRRORS it (same location, same load form) instead of inventing a parallel one. A false negative-existence claim makes the plan build duplicate/inconsistent infrastructure — the inverse of P1's phantom-symbol.

### P33
For any guard that DISAMBIGUATES by guessing among candidate parses/positions/interpretations, do NOT "deny if any candidate is dangerous" — that over-denies every input whose benign parse shares the shape. Deny ONLY on POSITIVE detection: an UNAMBIGUOUS resolution to a dangerous value. When the parse is ambiguous, fall to the safe NON-deny state — for a 3-way classifier that is ASK (human-gated), never DENY-on-a-guess and never silent ALLOW. Test the dominance property: the new rule must introduce ZERO over-deny relative to the prior behavior while only tightening positively-detected cases. The tell is a rationalization like "a false-positive deny is free / the string is malicious anyway" — verify a BENIGN input can't produce the same candidate shape before accepting it.

### P34
For every call site a plan calls "production" / "a production path" / "the production caller", open the file and confirm the call is NOT inside a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block (Rust), a `#[test]`/`#[cfg(test)]`-gated fn, an `if __name__ == "__main__"` / `tests/`-dir / pytest module (Python), or a `describe`/`it` test block (JS). Trace the actual production entrypoint (CLI `main`/command fn, server handler, public API) and verify it reaches the shared function — often it routes through ONE service method, not the call sites the plan enumerated. A call site under a test gate is test scaffolding: it may need a signature-compat update to compile, but it is NOT production wiring.

## C — Code checks

### C2
Before adding a "reject N" rule, grep every consumer of the field (`> 0`, `== 0`, `is_empty()`, `.unwrap_or`, match arms). If ANY consumer treats the rejected value as a sentinel/disable/default mode, do not reject it. A scan finding that says "reject 0" may be wrong about the value being invalid — verify against usage.

### C3
Any new/edited CI step invoking `maturin develop` or other venv-requiring tools must create + activate a venv first (`python -m venv .venv && . .venv/bin/activate` in the SAME `run:` block, since each step is a fresh shell — or persist via `$GITHUB_PATH`/`$GITHUB_ENV`). You usually can't run the job locally, so this is a read-time check.

### C4
For any feature whose safety story is "lossy inline view + full stash for retrieval," verify three things before dispatch: (a) **stash the RAW source**, not a display/filtered projection — trace what the projection drops and confirm the stashed bytes contain everything it lost; (b) **the retrieval pointer must survive truncation** — never append a load-bearing pointer to a body that is then head/tail-truncated; reserve budget (`cap.saturating_sub(note.len())`) and append the note AFTER the cap (or prepend it), with a test that a max-size body still ends/starts with the pointer; (c) **gate the destructive transform on stash SUCCESS** — if the backup write fails or is unavailable, fall back to the prior NON-destructive behavior (plain truncation keeping a contiguous prefix), so "transformed ⇒ retrievable" is an invariant, not a hope. Also: when adding the treatment to a success path, check whether the ERROR path needs it too (large error diagnostics are outputs), and gate the error path on ITS OWN lossy condition (its display cap actually dropping content), not a threshold copied from the success path.

### C5
For any test that touches a live external resource (real DB/index/filesystem-state/network/daemon — not an in-memory or temp fixture), it must NOT run in the default `cargo test`/`pytest`/CI suite. An "if absent, skip" guard is insufficient: it only covers the absent branch, so the test is non-hermetic exactly where the resource exists. Gate it behind `#[ignore]` / a feature flag / an env var so the default suite is deterministic regardless of local state, and keep the skip-guard for the explicit opt-in invocation. Ask: "if this resource IS present and slow, does the default suite still finish fast and deterministically?" If not, it's not a default-suite test. (This is RUNTIME hermeticity of a test that compiles fine — beware the inverse-masking trap: a locked/absent local resource makes it skip, hiding the hang from the author.)

### C10
For any guard that hand-models a command's option grammar, enumerate EACH modeled flag's COMPLETE spelling set — short `-x`, long `--alias`, `--long=value`, and combined `-xVALUE` — from the actual man page (don't model one spelling and assume the rest); a missed alias is a silent grammar hole. Separately, list the command's NON-EXEC / print-and-exit / operate-on-existing-PID / packed-string modes and route them to the SAFE-ambiguous state (ASK/None), never unwrap them as if a command follows. Verify the dominance property both ways: no catastrophe slips via an unmodeled common spelling (dilution), and no benign non-exec form newly denies (over-deny). Pairs with P33 (ambiguous→ask, not deny-on-guess) — C10 is the enumeration-completeness half: P33 says "don't guess," C10 says "model the whole real grammar so you don't have to." **Three spelling axes beyond short-vs-long that recur (Baxter 06-03, 3 Codex rounds): (1) CLUSTERED short-option bundles — a value/significant flag hidden mid-cluster (`bash -exc CMD`, `taskset -ac LIST CMD`, `watch -bt`) is NOT an exact-token match; decompose every single-dash cluster into its letters. (2) `--` END-OF-OPTIONS — model it explicitly: for a wrapper whose command follows (`watch -- CMD`, `taskset -- MASK CMD`) `--` means \"rest is the command\" → must still resolve/deny; for a flag-that-takes-a-string (`sh -- script`) it means \"rest is NOT options\". (3) OPTION-REGION TERMINATION — an option-scan hunting a flag (e.g. shell `-c`) must STOP at `--` and at the first non-option positional, else a later positional that looks like the flag (`bash script.sh -c CMD`, where `script.sh` is the script and `-c CMD` are its args) is mis-read → over-deny. Enumerate clustered + `--` + termination, not just short/long/=/combined.**

### C11
For any plan adding an alternative/override value consumed by existing ranking/selection/eviction code: (1) grep EVERY read of the old field across the selector(s) and confirm the effective-value reaches ALL of them — list the sites by file:line, don't say "the selection logic" (a missed site = partially-inert feature, the inverse of a no-op); (2) centralize as ONE accessor (`effective_x()`) used by all comparators, never hand-threaded `unwrap_or` per closure; (3) preserve the EXISTING tie-break semantics verbatim — if the code is "first-wins within EPSILON," keep first-wins (swap only the value read), do NOT swap to `max_by`/`sort` which reorders ties; (4) guarantee the new value is FINITE at the write boundary (clamp/reject NaN/Inf) before it enters any `>`/`partial_cmp` site, at a SINGLE sanitization boundary — do NOT split it across producer+caller with contradictory contracts (a producer that remaps `NaN→NEG_INFINITY` is still emitting a non-finite value whose doc claims "finite only"; pick one site, usually the caller, and have it reject non-finite → fall back to the old value); (5) the "byte-identical when off" claim needs a dedicated pre-feature-vs-feature-disabled equality test — an upstream golden (here `fuse()`) or a sibling-parity test does NOT cover the post-feature selector refactor, AND a run-to-run determinism / self-comparison test is ALSO insufficient (it compares post-change code to itself and misses regressions the new wiring introduced) — you must capture a FROZEN pre-edit baseline snapshot BEFORE any selector edit lands and assert exact equality against that constant. Pairs with P30 (read the helper BODY) and P31 (None-sentinel semantics): C11 is the write side — a new optional value flowing INTO existing comparators.

### C12
For any cross-process mutex in SQLite (or any check-then-act lock): (a) NEVER release/reclaim with an unconditional `DELETE WHERE <pk>` — scope every delete to the identity you hold/observed (lease token for release; observed `owner_pid`+`locked_at` for reclaim) so it's a compare-and-swap, not a blind wipe; (b) do NOT use `setInterval` to keep a lock alive across a SYNCHRONOUS critical section — the event loop is blocked, the timer is dead code (refresh inline at work checkpoints, or don't rely on refresh); (c) prefer LIVENESS (owner PID + `kill(pid,0)`) over a wall-clock TTL — a fixed TTL either evicts a live long-running owner (overlap) or strands a crash (pick your failure), whereas liveness never evicts a live owner and reclaims a dead one immediately; keep a generous TTL only as a documented PID-reuse backstop, and even then gate it so it can't override a confirmed-live owner; (d) make table creation `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` so two first-runs on an upgraded DB don't race on the check-then-create. Note: in a single process better-sqlite3 is synchronous so same-process callers serialize — the races are all CROSS-process (multiple daemons/CLIs on one DB file), which is exactly when the lock matters.

### C13
For any default-flip / new behavior-gate, grep EVERY site that performs the gated side effect (here: every vault-mutating call — `moveToTrash`/`writeFile`/`unlink`/`rename`), not just the primary loop, and confirm each is under the SAME gate (or is genuinely in the other direction / DB-only). Enumerate the rare branches explicitly: deletions, tombstones, cleanup/GC passes, conflict paths, AND the dry-run/preview counters (a preview that reports an action the real run now skips is itself a bug). Write the invariant test to drive each side-branch (delete-on-X, tag-gains-flag, dry-run), not only create/update — the headline invariant ("pull = zero writes", "read-only mode", "feature-flag off") is only as true as its least-traveled branch.

### C14
For any allow-list hardening (auto-approve → ask on a dangerous flag): **(axis 1)** enumerate EVERY allowlisted command, not just the obvious ones — for EACH, check its real man/help for exec, arbitrary-file-WRITE (`-o`/`--output`/compile/profile), and indirect-out-of-root-READ (`--files-from`/`--files0-from`/list-of-paths) flags; missing a whole command is the easiest gap. **(axis 2)** a value-taking SHORT flag must be matched by "single-dash cluster CONTAINS the letter" (catches `-oV`, bundled `-rno`), never exact `== "-o"`; a long flag needs both `--x` and `--x=` (cf. C10). **(axis 3)** if a command is an INTERPRETER or has an open-ended/version-growing exec-flag surface, do NOT blocklist its exec flags (whack-a-mole that loses on the next version) — use a positive WHITELIST: allow ONLY the one known-safe invocation shape, parsed STATEFULLY by argument position (options end at `--` or the first positional; a flag-looking token after that is a filename, not a flag). Direction must be fail-safe (allow→ask only) so every miss degrades to a human gate, never to silent allow. Pairs with C10 (flag-spelling completeness) and P33 (fail toward the safe state).

### C15
For any migration/parity test: (1) build the legacy fixture as a VERBATIM copy of the real pre-migration DDL — every column default, every index, every trigger, every constraint — not a simplified subset; seed data that the OLD constraints permit, and exercise the NEW constraint's relaxation only AFTER the migration runs (insert the now-legal duplicate post-migration). (2) A schema-parity comparison must cover `dflt_value` (not just name/type/notnull) AND every non-table object (indexes, triggers) via normalized `sqlite_master.sql`. (3) Never reason "`notnull` proves the default matches" — `ADD COLUMN NOT NULL DEFAULT x` and `CREATE ... NOT NULL` both report `notnull=1` but differ on `dflt_value`; the default is the drift axis a column-shape check misses. Pairs with C21-class dual-schema parity (multiple DDL sources must converge).

### C16
When deciding whether a table needs a `graph_id`/tenant column, do NOT accept "an FK already bounds it" — FK CASCADE governs row deletion on parent-delete, not query scope. Ask the access questions instead: does any SELECT return rows across tenants? does any UPDATE/DELETE address a row by a GLOBAL id (autoincrement PK) without a tenant predicate? If yes to either, the table needs the scope column and every query needs the predicate — it is in scope, not deferrable. A table slated for future removal is still a live hole until removed. Enumerate ALL tenant-access paths, not only repo/manager query methods — schema-level triggers (AFTER INSERT, AFTER UPDATE) and scheduled prune/GC/retention jobs that SELECT or DELETE without a tenant predicate are cross-tenant holes a method-by-method audit walks past; e.g. an unscoped `AFTER INSERT` prune trigger (`DELETE ... OFFSET N` with no tenant predicate) is a cross-tenant DELETE invisible to a method enumeration. Pairs with C13 (default-flip leaves a side-branch ungated) — both are "the headline isolation/invariant is only as true as its least-scoped access path."

### C17
For any helper that tests path EQUALITY/identity by suffix, require SEGMENT ALIGNMENT: after normalizing, accept exact-eq OR a suffix match where the character before the matched suffix in the longer path is a `/` (e.g. `long.strip_suffix(short).is_some_and(|p| p.is_empty() || p.ends_with('/'))`), bidirectionally if either side may be absolute. Add NEGATIVE tests for unaligned collisions (`foobar.rs`↔`bar.rs`) and different-dir same-basename (`a/b.rs`↔`c/b.rs`), not just the positive absolute-vs-relative case. Audit the fan-out: a suffix matcher that cross-references against MANY candidates is far higher-risk than a single membership test — "a sibling helper already does it this way" does not transfer when the fan-out differs. Pairs with C1 (path trust boundary — there the risk is traversal/bypass; here it's silent over-match in attribution/classification).

### C18
For any metric whose REPORTED unit is coarse (file/module/package/user) but whose IMPLEMENTATION matches/keys/filters on a FINER unit (symbol/line/row/session): confirm the coarse rollup cannot be starved — would the coarse unit's signal be MISSED if the specific fine unit that survives filtering/dedup/budget is not the one carrying it? Match at the metric's OWN granularity (test FILE membership, not exact-symbol identity). Then identify the DIRECTION of any residual mis-aggregation and verify it does NOT bias toward the author's hypothesis / a catch-all bucket. Add a test where the surviving fine-unit differs from the signal-carrying fine-unit within the same coarse unit. Pairs with C17 (segment-aligned path matching for the file test) and P31 (don't infer a unit's class from its empty/partial output).

### C19
For any plan/diff that adds a guard/deny-case/match-arm to an existing dispatch function, TRACE the function top-to-bottom for the inputs the new arm targets and confirm NO earlier branch `return`s first: list every `return`/early-exit ABOVE the new arm (wrapper unwraps, recursion interceptors, `if let Some(..) { return }`, higher `match` arms, guard clauses) and check whether each fires for the new arm's target inputs. If an earlier branch preempts them, the new arm is dead — either move it earlier, fold the logic into the preempting branch, or correct the plan's claim (and any corpus/assertion row) to the ACTUAL post-dispatch behavior. A "new-deny" / "no-regress" row must be validated by running the classifier (or hand-tracing the full dispatch order), not by reading the target arm alone. Pairs with C13 (there an existing side-branch keeps the OLD behavior; here a new arm never RUNS) and P30 (read the body, not the signature — here: read the control-flow ORDER, not just the arm).

### C20
For any test (in a plan spec OR a diff) that asserts an entity is PRESENT / surfaced / ranked / returned, trace the data path: what input must the fixture provide for that entity to reach the asserted output? Seed-the-named-symbols is not enough when the asserted behavior depends on a graph edge, a foreign-key relation, an embedding, a config flag, an event subscription, or a prior write. If the fixture provides no such route, the test is vacuous — flag it and add the missing seed (or assert on the reachable signal instead). Ask: "with exactly this fixture, by what concrete mechanism does the asserted entity appear?" If you cannot name the mechanism, the test cannot pass honestly. Beware the silent-weakening trap: a vacuous presence-assert often gets softened to a tautology during implementation.

### C21
For any write/stamp/claim that LOCATES rows by a key and relies on single-row semantics (`rowCount === 1`/`=== 0`, a `persisted`/`claimed`/`updated` boolean derived from rowcount, or a "find-by-X returns at most one" assumption), open the SCHEMA and confirm a UNIQUE constraint/index actually ENFORCES that key's uniqueness — an "expected unique" comment, a `RETURNING id` on a presumed-single row, or a "the provider mints globally-unique ids" rationale is NOT enforcement. If the table is multi-tenant, the UNIQUE key MUST include the tenant column (`UNIQUE (tenant_col, key)`); a bare-key unique constraint either leaks across tenants or rejects a legitimate same-key row in another tenant. For a PARTIAL index, the conflict/predicate must be restated. If the locating key is not uniquely enforced, the single-row assertion is unsound: make it UNIQUE, or handle `rowCount > 1` explicitly (never let a boolean silently misreport). Pairs with C16 (tenant-isolation — there the gap is a missing scope PREDICATE on reads; here it's missing uniqueness ENFORCEMENT on the scoped write key) and the tenant-scoped-idempotency-key memory.

### C22
For any resolver/guard with a PRIMARY typed-column lookup + a FALLBACK, confirm the primary value is SHAPE-VALIDATED before the typed query: a `uuid` needs a UUID-regex/`isUuid` guard, an `int` needs an integer parse, an `enum` needs membership — when the guard FAILS, skip straight to the fallback, never let the malformed value reach the typed comparison. Trace the failure path: "if the primary value is garbage, does control reach the fallback, or does the DB driver throw first?" If the column is strongly typed and the input is external free text, it throws first unless guarded. Add a test with a MALFORMED primary value + a valid fallback value asserting the fallback resolves (no throw, no internal-error). Binding the value as a parameter does NOT save you — `text = uuid` still casts and throws; the guard must be in app code BEFORE the query. Pairs with P33/C10 (fail toward the safe/recoverable state, not a hard error) and the verify-before-trust baseline (an external id is untrusted shape AND untrusted ownership).

### C23
1. If the diff adds a `CREATE ROLE` / `ALTER ROLE` for a narrow role, confirm the migration also
   `REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON <fenced table>` + `… ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public` + `… ON SCHEMA
   public` FROM the role BEFORE re-granting only the intended columns (table-level REVOKE clears
   column privileges too). Idempotent least-privilege even on a dirty role.
2. If a boot guard validates the role, confirm it connects THROUGH the actual pool and asserts
   `current_user = session_user = <role>`, `pg_auth_members` count = 0 **in BOTH directions**, and live
   `has_column_privilege`(fenced)=false + (allowed)=true — not just a `pg_roles` name/attribute check.
   **BOTH directions matters:** (a) roles the fenced role is a MEMBER OF (`m.member = <role>` — inherits
   app_user's tenant DML); AND (b) roles that are MEMBERS OF the fenced role (`m.roleid = <role>`) — the
   more dangerous, easily-missed direction: `GRANT <fenced_role> TO app_user` makes the fenced role's
   permissive `… USING (true)` RLS policy APPLY to app_user (Postgres applies a `TO role` policy to inherited
   members; permissive policies OR → all rows) → full cross-tenant read. A guard checking only `m.member`
   passes this attack (Codex caught it on Mabel M3 email webhook `email_webhook_resolver`, 2026-07-02).
3. Confirm that guard runs BEFORE any consumer that uses the pool is constructed (queue Workers
   consume on construction).

### C24
1. If the diff enqueues with a stable/custom `jobId` for dedup, confirm `removeOnFail: true` (not a
   retained count) so a failed job frees the key for the next poll to re-enqueue — plus `attempts` +
   `backoff` so a transient failure self-heals before removal. Ask: "if this job fails ONCE, does the
   key free up so the producer can re-enqueue?" If no → permanent silent wedge.
2. If the diff calls `upsertJobScheduler` / adds a repeatable/cron job, confirm the jobTemplate carries
   `opts: { removeOnComplete: true, removeOnFail: <bounded> }` — unbounded retention is a Redis leak.

### C25
For every REQUIRED validation/teaching-error the spec names (a "must", a "reject when", a "needs >= N"), trace the control flow and confirm NO earlier early-return (empty-result, no-op, default-success, cache-hit, "nothing to do") can intercept the SAME inputs the gate is supposed to reject — especially the zero/empty boundary. If an early success-return precedes a required gate, move the gate before the return (or make the return conditional on the gate having passed). Add an explicit test at the degenerate boundary (0 items, empty set), not just the near-boundary (1 item).

### C26
for any test that claims to PIN a whole config/payload/object ("asserts every field", "proven-teeth", "all audited knobs") by asserting fields one-by-one: does it ALSO assert the exact top-level key SET (a `Object.keys(...).sort()` toEqual, a full-object `toEqual`/`toStrictEqual`, or an explicit absence list that is provably COMPLETE)? If it only asserts the fields it names, ADDING a new key or LEAKING a key from a sibling branch passes silently — the teeth are one-directional. Flag it and add the key-set pin. Two amplifiers to check: (1) if the value's type is `Partial<T>`/an open interface AND it reaches the sink via a spread or a variable (not a fresh object literal at the return), tsc's excess-property check does NOT fire, so the type is NOT a backstop for additions; (2) an absence/omission list (`expect("x" in obj).toBe(false)`) is only as strong as its completeness — enumerate the sibling branch's full key set and diff, don't hand-pick a few.
